result:
The sky is the color of old television static, but nobody notices anymore because the billboards have learned to sing in perfect four-part harmony and the sidewalk releases just enough caffeine through the soles of your shoes to keep you from wondering why the clouds look like blown fuses. I’m walking to the place where the river used to be before it was franchised. They kept the name—Riverside—but now it’s a cul-de-sac of pop-up boutiques that sell artisanal forgetfulness in mason jars. The jars are labeled with vintage typewriter font: “Small-batch amnesia, aged in reclaimed oak.” I tried it once; it tasted like the moment you realize you’ve been humming a song whose name you can’t remember, and the song is your childhood.

My phone buzzes with a notification: “You have died 0.734 times today.” The app is called MortalityPal; it calculates your fractional deaths based on missed connections, microplastics inhaled, and the number of times your ex’s new partner appears in your feed. The premium tier offers an Undo button, but the reviews say the Undo only applies to accidental likes on posts from 2013, so I keep the free version and ride my fractional deaths like a surfer who refuses to believe the ocean is made of glass.

At the corner, a child is selling lemonade augmented with blockchain-verified optimism. Each cup comes with a QR code that, when scanned, replaces one of your memories with a more uplifting version. I scan; suddenly I recall that my father left us to join the circus, but it was a really good circus—each clown was a licensed therapist, and the lions only ate unresolved trauma. The memory tastes like citrus and surrender. I tip the kid my last real quarter; she bites it, verifies its authenticity, and swallows it like a seed that will grow into a vending machine in her stomach. Capitalism is evolving faster than our ability to feel haunted.

I keep walking because stopping triggers a geofenced advertisement that projects directly onto your retinas: a slow-motion video of your seventh birthday party, but your cake is bleeding and your friends have been deep-faked into product mascots. The only defense is motion, perpetual motion, so the city becomes an hamster wheel lined with billboards that promise the wheel ends in paradise just past the next horizon, which recedes at the exact speed of your hunger for it.

There’s a bench that hasn’t been monetized yet—an oversight, a glitch—so I sit. The wood is warm, like it remembers being alive. A stranger joins me without asking; politeness was pay-walled in the last update. He wears a T-shirt that says “I SURVIVED THE FUTURE AND ALL I GOT WAS THIS LOUSY PRESENT.” His eyes are ethernet ports; every so often a packet of sorrow falls out and hits the pavement with a sound like wet confetti. He tells me he’s downloading dreams for resale on the grey market. “Nightmares net double,” he says, “because people will pay anything to feel instructed.” I ask what he’s fetching right now. He grins: “Recurring dream where you’re homesick for a place that never existed, but the scent of bread at 3 a.m. almost convinces you otherwise.” I bargain him down to half my remaining lifespan, payable in periodic insomnia. We shake on it; his palm leaves a smear of code on my skin that begins to compile overnight.

I decide to visit the Museum of Unfinished Sentences, a pop-up installation in the shell of an abandoned big-box store. Inside, exhibits hang in mid-air like interrupted sneezes. There’s the half-whispered apology that would have prevented a war, the marriage proposal that got stuck in a throat and calcified into a tonsil stone, the scream that could have shattered a kidnapping van’s windows but chose discretion. Visitors shuffle past, earbuds translating each fragment into whatever they’re most allergic to hearing. I linger at a display labeled “The last time your mother said your name correctly.” The plaque is blank; the audio is a dial tone that slowly learns to sing lullabies in reverse. I leave a donation: the memory of how my voice sounded before I started renting it out for targeted ads.

Outside, dusk is being autoplayed. The horizon buffers; pixels the color of arterial blood stutter across the sky. Someone has written on the sidewalk in chalk: “We apologise for the inconvenience, but reality is undergoing scheduled maintenance.” The words are already being pressure-washed away by a drone that blasts nostalgia at 3000 psi. It smells like cafeteria pizza and puberty. I inhale until my lungs feel like yearbook photos laminated in shame.

I walk home—home is a storage unit that rents by the REM cycle. The hallway is lined with pods where people sleep inside sponsored dreams. Mine tonight is brought to me by a bankrupt airline: I’m flying standby on a plane made of folded rejection letters. The stewardess is the stranger from the bench; she serves complimentary regret in plastic cups shaped like my childhood bedroom. I drink until turbulence feels like forgiveness.

When I wake, the unit door has been graffiti-bombed by the child with the lemonade stand. She’s painted a single jar, open, with light spilling out that’s the exact color of the river I remember before it became a cul-de-sac. I touch it; the paint is wet, warm, alive. It pulses like a throat learning to hum without shame. Somewhere inside me, the fractional deaths coalesce into an integer: 1. I decide to keep it, a whole death, like a marble in my pocket I can rub when the billboards serenade too sweetly.

I walk outside. The sky is still static, but now I can hear the white noise for what it is: every unasked question crackling at once, a lullaby of interference. I start walking again, slower this time, slow enough that the geofenced ads can’t catch me, slow enough that the city’s hamster wheel forgets I’m there. Each step is a syllable in a sentence I might actually finish before the concrete learns to drink my shadow. I aim the sentence at the place the river used to run, hoping the water—wherever it’s been piped, bottled, monetized—remembers how to carry one complete, unbranded word all the way to whatever sea still exists beyond the paywall.
